# Phase 3: The Trunk (Forms, Templates & HTMX) 🌳

While producing JSON APIs is crucial, Eden shines brightest as a full-stack framework. In this phase, we master the "Trunk" of our application: the user interface. 

Eden merges **Pydantic Validation**, **Modern Template Directives**, and **HTMX Interactivity** to build dynamic Single Page Applications (SPAs) without writing a single line of custom JavaScript.

---

## 🏗️ 1. Pydantic-Powered Forms

Let's start by safely accepting user input. Eden's `BaseForm` bridges the gap between Python types, HTML rendering, and validation.

### Generating Forms from Resources

If you are using the `Resource` pattern from Phase 2, form creation is incredibly integrated.

```python
from eden.resources import Resource
from eden.db import f

class Task(Resource):
    title: Mapped[str] = f()
    completed: Mapped[bool] = f(default=False)
```

In your view logic, you can easily access an empty or populated form:

```python
# Create an empty form structure for creation
form = Task.get_form()

# Create a form pre-populated with an existing instance's data for editing
existing_task = await Task.get(id="...")
form = existing_task.Form
```

### Manual Forms with `BaseModel`

You are not tied to the database! You can use raw Pydantic schemas to construct entirely custom logic forms (like login, filters, or search forms):

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from eden.forms import BaseForm

class ContactSchema(BaseModel):
    name: str
    message: str

# Inside your route
async def contact_post(request):
    # BaseForm automatically handles standard layout data OR multipart uploads
    form = await BaseForm.from_multipart(ContactSchema, request)
    
    if form.is_valid():
        print(f"Validated! {form.model_instance.name}")
        # Proceed with business logic...
    else:
        # Re-render the form. Form errors are gracefully attached to `form.errors`
        return request.app.render("contact.html", {"form": form})
```

---

## 🎨 2. Form Rendering in Templates

Eden templates give you immense flexibility in how you display forms. Fields automatically receive HTML attributes (like `required`) based on your Pydantic boundaries.

### Method 1: The Magic `@render_field`

The absolute fastest way to render a field. This directive outputs the complete package: the `<label>`, the actual `<input>` (or `<textarea>`, `<select>`), and any `<span class="error">` validation messages underneath.

```html
<form method="POST">
    @csrf
    
    <!-- Automatically infers it's a Text input -->
    @render_field(form['title'], class="input-modern", placeholder="Task Title")
    
    <button type="submit">Save File</button>
</form>
```

### Method 2: High-Control Individual Rendering

For complex CSS designs, you might not want the composite wrapper. You can access methods on individual form fields via `#{{ form['field_name'] }}`.

```html
<form method="POST">
    @csrf
    
    <div class="custom-layout">
        <div class="label-wrapper">
            {{ form['description'].render_label()|safe }}
        </div>
        
        <div class="input-wrapper">
            <!-- Explicit widget overrides -->
            {{ form['description'].as_textarea(rows="4", class="textarea-premium") }}
            
            @if(form['description'].error) {
                <p class="text-red-500 font-bold mb-2">{{ form['description'].error }}</p>
            }
        </div>
    </div>
</form>
```

**Available Field Modifiers:**
Eden's `FormField` includes several `.as_XXX()` casting methods and CSS modifiers to build precise UIs:
- `{{ form['field'].render(class="foo") }}`: Standard text input rendering.
- `{{ form['field'].as_hidden() }}`: `<input type="hidden">`
- `{{ form['field'].as_textarea(rows=5) }}`: Multi-line `<textarea>`
- `{{ form['field'].as_select(choices) }}`: `<select>` dropdown.
- `{{ form['field'].as_file(accept='image/*') }}`: File upload input.
- `{{ form['field'].add_class("font-bold") }}`: Appends Tailwind classes cleanly.
- `{{ form['field'].add_error_class("border-red-500") }}`: Applies a class *only* if the field failed Pydantic validation.

### Method 3: Iterate and Automatic `render_all()`

If you just need a functional admin screen immediately:
```html
<form method="POST">
    @csrf
    {{ form.render_all()|safe }}
    <button>Submit</button>
</form>
```
Or iterate over them for custom layout rules:
```html
@for(field in form) {
    <div class="col-span-12">
        @render_field(field)
    </div>
}
```

---

## ✨ 3. Modern Templating: Eden Directives

Eden extends Jinja2 with a fast, modern "Directive" syntax heavily inspired by Blade and modern frontend frameworks. This cleans up templating significantly, removing the clunky `{% %}` syntax for logic.

### Structural Directives

Instead of verbose inheritance `{% extends 'base.html' %}`, you write:

```html
@extends("base.html")

<!-- @section acts as your content blocks -->
@section("content") {
    <h1>Welcome to the Sector</h1>
}

<!-- Use @include to pull in sub-templates quickly -->
@include("partials/navbar.html")
```

### Control Flow

Notice the usage of scope braces `{}` rather than `endif` / `endfor`.

```html
<!-- Conditionals -->
@if(user.is_admin) {
    <button>Delete Sector</button>
} @else if(user.role == 'manager') {
    <button>Archive</button>
} @else {
    <button disabled>Restricted</button>
}

<!-- Loops. The 'as' keyword is fully supported! -->
<ul>
@for(task in tasks) {
    <li>{{ task.title }}</li>
} @empty {
    <li>No tasks found today!</li>
}
</ul>
```

### Security & State Checks

```html
<!-- Auth checks -->
@auth {
    <p>Welcome back, {{ request.user.email }}</p>
}
@guest {
    <a href="/login">Please Sign In</a>
}

<!-- Generate URLs effortlessly. 'core_dashboard' resolves mapped endpoints -->
<a href="@url('core_dashboard')">Home</a>

<!-- Active Link Checks - Applies the class ONLY if the current URL matches the route -->
<a href="@url('tasks')" @active_link('tasks', 'border-b-2 border-blue-500')>
    View Tasks
</a>
```

### Component Architecture (`@component`, `@slot`)

Eden possesses a rich component system allowing you to build reusable UI elements without duplicating HTML. 

**Define the Component (`components/card.html`):**
```html
<div class="bg-gray-800 shadow-lg rounded-xl p-6">
    <div class="card-header text-blue-500 font-bold">
        <!-- A named slot -->
        @slot("header") {
            Default Title
        }
    </div>
    
    <div class="card-body">
        <!-- The default anonymous slot -->
        @yield("content")
    </div>
</div>
```

**Use the Component (`dashboard.html`):**
```html
@component("components/card.html", class="mt-4") {
    
    <!-- Injecting into the named slot -->
    @slot("header") {
        <h3>Server Metrics</h3>
    }
    
    <!-- Everything else falls into the anonymous slot -->
    <p>CPU Usage: 45%</p>
    <p>Memory: 1.2GB</p>
}
```

---

## ⚡ 4. HTMX Integration: The Premium SPA Experience

Eden treats HTTP requests as capable UI events. We leverage HTMX heavily to achieve Single Page Application (SPA) reactivity without bulky frontend JavaScript payloads like React or Vue.

Eden is "HTMX-Native". It automatically detects HTMX requests and provides a fluent API for controlling the client from the server.

### 4.1 The `HtmxResponse` Fluent API

When returning responses from a server action, `HtmxResponse` gives you chainable methods to control the browser directly from your Python route.

```python
from eden.htmx import HtmxResponse, is_htmx
from eden import Router

router = Router()

@router.post("/tasks/{id}/toggle")
async def toggle_task(request, id: str):
    task = await Task.get(id=id)
    await task.update(completed=not task.completed)
    
    # Standard HTML response
    html = f"<button class='success'>{'Done' if task.completed else 'Pending'}</button>"
    
    if is_htmx(request):
        # We can control the browser directly from the server!
        return (
            HtmxResponse(html)
            .trigger("taskToggled", {"task_id": id}) # Fires a JS event on the client
            .swap("outerHTML")                       # Instructs HTMX how to swap the HTML
            .retarget(f"#task-btn-{id}")             # Changes where the HTML is injected
            # .hx_redirect("/success")               # (Optional) Native client-side redirect
        )
    return html
```

### 4.2 The Auto-Fragment Power (`@fragment`)

What if you have a massive page, but you only want to update a small table inside it when an HTMX request is triggered?

In other frameworks, you have to write *two* routes: one for the full page, one for the partial table. **Not in Eden.**

**In your template (`dashboard.html`):**

```html
@extends("base.html")

<div class="sidebar">...</div>

<div class="main-content">
    <!-- Notice this fragment block wrapper -->
    @fragment("table_chunk") {
        <table id="task-table">
            @for(task in tasks) {
                <tr><td>{{ task.title }}</td></tr>
            }
        </table>
    }
</div>
```

**In your view:**
```python
@router.get("/dashboard")
async def dashboard(request):
    tasks = await Task.all()
    # 1. If a standard request hits this, it renders the FULL page.
    # 2. If an HTMX request specifies `hx-target="#task-table"`, OR explicitly passes
    #    the fragment name in the context, Eden automatically intercepts it and ONLY
    #    renders the "table_chunk" fragment, saving huge amounts of bandwidth!
    return request.app.render("dashboard.html", {"tasks": tasks})
```

---

### 4.3 Practical Example: Active Search

Here is how you build a lightning-fast live search that filters a table as the user types, using Eden and HTMX.

**The Search Input (HTML):**
```html
<!-- 
  hx-get: Where to send the request
  hx-target: Where to put the results
  hx-trigger: Fire on 'keyup', but wait 300ms (debounce) to avoid spamming the server
-->
<input type="search" 
       name="q" 
       placeholder="Search tasks..." 
       class="input-premium"
       hx-get="/tasks/search" 
       hx-trigger="keyup changed delay:300ms, search" 
       hx-target="#search-results">

<div id="search-results">
    <!-- Results inject here -->
</div>
```

**The Server Route (Python):**
```python
@router.get("/tasks/search")
async def search_tasks(request, q: str = ""):
    # Query your Eden Models
    tasks = await Task.filter(Task.title.ilike(f"%{q}%"))
    
    # Return just a partial fragment!
    return request.app.render("partials/task_list.html", {"tasks": tasks})
```

### 4.4 Practical Example: Infinite Scroll

To build infinite scroll (like Twitter or Instagram) without JavaScript, you just trigger an HTMX request when the last element enters the viewport.

**The Template (`partials/task_list.html`):**
```html
@for(task in tasks) {
    <!-- 
      For the LAST item in the loop, we add HTMX attributes.
      'revealed' means HTMX fires when this element scrolls into view.
      'hx-swap="afterend"' appends the new results to the bottom of the list.
    -->
    @if(loop.last and has_more_pages) {
        <div class="task-card"
             hx-get="/tasks?page={{ current_page + 1 }}"
             hx-trigger="revealed"
             hx-swap="afterend">
            {{ task.title }}
        </div>
    } @else {
        <div class="task-card">
            {{ task.title }}
        </div>
    }
}
```

### 4.5 Practical Example: Inline Form Validation

You can validate Pydantic forms instantly as the user types.

**The Form:**
```html
<form hx-post="/users/create" hx-target="this" hx-swap="outerHTML">
    @csrf
    
    <!-- Validate the email field when the user clicks away (blur) -->
    <div hx-target="this" hx-swap="outerHTML">
        <label>Email</label>
        <input type="email" name="email" value="{{ form['email'].value }}"
               hx-post="/users/validate-email">
        
        @if(form['email'].error) {
            <span class="text-red-500 text-sm">{{ form['email'].error }}</span>
        }
    </div>
    
    <button type="submit">Create User</button>
</form>
```

**The Server Route:**
```python
@router.post("/users/validate-email")
async def validate_email(request):
    # BaseForm can validate single fields out of a larger schema
    form = await BaseForm.from_multipart(UserSchema, request)
    
    # Even if other fields are missing, we only care if 'email' is valid for this check
    form.validate_field('email') 
    
    # Return just the Email input HTML snippet (with or without the error span!)
    return request.app.render("partials/email_input.html", {"form": form})
```

---

### 🎉 Phase 3 Complete

You now have a deep understanding of standardizing input with Pydantic Forms, writing clean UIs with Eden Template Directives, and supercharging interactivity with HTMX!

Your frontend logic is **100% Verified**. Next, we lock down our system to secure our sectors.

**Up Next: [Phase 4: The Branches (Security, RBAC & Isolation)](./phase-4.md)**
